questions.q: Define work in physics and identify its unit.
options: The effort exerted, measured in newtons
Force times displacement in the direction of the force, measured in joules
Force times time, measured in watts
Mass times velocity, measured in joules
questions.explanation: Work is the product of the force and the displacement in the direction of the force (W = Fs), a scalar measured in joules. The everyday sense of 'work' as effort is the trap — physically, if there is no displacement (or the force is perpendicular to the motion), no work is done however hard you push.

questions.q: Explain why no work is done when a person holds a heavy box stationary.
options: There is no displacement, and work requires movement in the direction of the force
The force is doubled by holding it
questions.explanation: Work requires the object to be displaced in the direction of the force. A stationary box has zero displacement, so the work done on it is zero regardless of how large the supporting force is. Similarly, a force perpendicular to the motion (like carrying a tray horizontally) does no work.

options: The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy
Work equals the gravitational potential energy gained
questions.explanation: The work-energy theorem states that the net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy. Positive net work speeds an object up; negative net work (e.g. friction) slows it down. It is a shortcut that gives final speed over a distance without computing acceleration or time.

questions.q: A ball is dropped from 5 m. Using conservation of energy, calculate its speed just before impact (g = 9.8 m s⁻², ignore air resistance).
questions.explanation: Potential energy lost = kinetic energy gained: mgΔh = ½mv². The mass cancels, so v = √(2gΔh) = √(2 × 9.8 × 5) = √98 ≈ 9.9 m s⁻¹. The speed depends only on the height dropped, not the mass — the energy version of 'all objects fall at the same rate'.

questions.q: A student says 'the car's kinetic energy is lost when it brakes to a stop.' Justify why this phrasing is incorrect.
options: The energy is correct as stated — it is lost
The kinetic energy is transformed into heat (and sound) by friction, not destroyed; energy is conserved
The kinetic energy becomes potential energy
Energy can be destroyed during braking
questions.explanation: By the law of conservation of energy, energy cannot be destroyed, only transformed. Braking converts the car's kinetic energy into thermal energy (heating the brakes) and a little sound. Markers specifically penalise 'lost' or 'used up' — the correct phrasing is 'transformed', and this is the single most important piece of energy language in the course.

questions.q: A net force of 100 N acts on a 10 kg object over 20 m, starting from rest. Using the work-energy theorem, calculate the final speed.
questions.explanation: Net work = Fs = 100 × 20 = 2000 J, which equals the kinetic energy gained. So ½mv² = 2000 → v² = 4000/10 = 400 → v = 20 m s⁻¹. This finds the final speed without ever computing the acceleration or time — the work-energy theorem as a shortcut.

questions.q: A block slides down a frictionless ramp. Identify what its speed at the bottom depends on.
options: The length of the slope and the mass
Only the vertical height descended (not the angle or mass)
Only the angle of the slope
The mass and the angle together
questions.explanation: By conservation of energy, mgΔh = ½mv², so v = √(2gΔh) — the mass cancels and only the vertical height matters. A steep short slope and a gentle long slope to the same vertical drop give the same final speed, because the same potential energy is converted. This is awkward with forces but obvious with energy.

questions.q: A car travels at a constant 28 m s⁻¹ against a total resistance of 700 N. Calculate the power the engine must deliver.
questions.explanation: At constant speed there is no acceleration, so the driving force equals the resistance (700 N). Using P = Fv = 700 × 28 = 19 600 W (≈20 kW). The power just replaces the energy being transformed to heat by air and rolling resistance. This uses the often-forgotten P = Fv form.

questions.q: A crane lifts a 500 kg load 20 m in 10 s at constant speed. Calculate the power output (g = 9.8 m s⁻²).
questions.explanation: Work done against gravity = mgΔh = 500 × 9.8 × 20 = 98 000 J. Power = work/time = 98 000/10 = 9800 W (9.8 kW). 'Constant speed' means kinetic energy doesn't change, so all the work goes into gravitational potential energy. 98 000 W would be the work mistaken for power (forgetting to divide by time).

questions.q: A 4 kg trolley moving at 6 m s⁻¹ on a frictionless surface runs up a ramp. Calculate the maximum vertical height reached (g = 9.8 m s⁻²).
questions.explanation: All the kinetic energy converts to potential energy at the top: ½mv² = mgΔh. The mass cancels, so Δh = v²/(2g) = 36/19.6 ≈ 1.84 m. If the ramp were rough, friction would transform some kinetic energy into heat, leaving less for potential energy, so the trolley would reach a lower height.
